course_info.title: Software Engineering
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Understand why software engineering discipline is necessary for large systems
Compare process models: Waterfall, V-Model, Spiral, and iterative approaches
Identify the phases of the software development lifecycle and their outputs
Evaluate trade-offs between plan-driven and adaptive development processes
readings: Sommerville, Software Engineering, 10th Ed., Ch. 1-2
Brooks, The Mythical Man-Month, Ch. 1 (The Tar Pit)
Royce, 'Managing the Development of Large Software Systems' (1970 paper)

lecture_notes.introduction: Writing code is the easy part of software engineering. The hard part is building systems that work reliably, can be maintained by teams of changing membership, and can be extended over years without collapsing under their own weight. The history of software is littered with catastrophic failures that were not caused by bad algorithms or incorrect syntax, but by failures of process: unclear requirements, untested assumptions, unmanaged complexity, and poor communication. The Waterfall model, introduced by Winston Royce in 1970 and frequently misunderstood, was never intended as a recommendation for sequential development. Royce actually argued for iterative refinement with feedback loops. The V-Model pairs each development phase with a corresponding testing phase, making verification explicit. The Spiral model adds risk analysis at each iteration. These models are not historical curiosities; understanding them reveals the recurring problems that every process model attempts to solve: how do we know we are building the right thing, how do we know we are building it correctly, and how do we manage the inevitable changes that occur during development? This course will equip you with the practices, tools, and judgment to navigate these questions on real projects.
key_concepts: The software crisis: cost overruns, schedule delays, and defects that motivated SE as a discipline, starting from the 1968 NATO conference
Waterfall model: sequential phases (requirements, design, implementation, testing, maintenance) with document-driven handoffs between phases
V-Model: maps each development phase to a testing phase (unit test validates detailed design, integration test validates architectural design, acceptance test validates requirements)
Spiral model: iterative development with explicit risk analysis at each cycle, combining prototyping with systematic planning
Iterative and incremental development: delivering working software in short cycles, enabling feedback and course correction before the full system is built

Plan-driven vs adaptive: plan-driven approaches invest heavily in upfront specification (appropriate when requirements are stable and cost of change is high), adaptive approaches embrace change (appropriate when requirements are uncertain)
Technical debt: the accumulated cost of shortcuts, workarounds, and deferred quality work that compounds over time as new code builds on top of compromised foundations
Process maturity: CMMI levels as a framework for organizational process improvement, from ad-hoc (Level 1) to optimizing (Level 5)
The iron triangle: scope, time, and cost are interdependent constraints; fixing all three guarantees quality problems
Software entropy: without active maintenance and refactoring, software quality degrades with every change, a phenomenon Lehman's laws of software evolution describe formally
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a real-world project failure: the FBI Virtual Case File system ($170M abandoned). Analyze how sequential development without user feedback, scope creep without requirements management, and contractor misalignment led to complete project cancellation. Then contrast with the recovery project (Sentinel) that succeeded by switching to Agile with shorter feedback loops and incremental delivery.
setup_requirements: Git installed and GitHub account configured with SSH keys
Project management tool access (GitHub Projects or Jira)
IDE of choice installed (IntelliJ, VS Code, or PyCharm)
Terminal/shell environment configured

review.value: Every shortcut taken today costs more to fix tomorrow. Like financial debt, it compounds: new code is built on top of the shortcut, making future changes increasingly expensive. The interest rate depends on how central the affected code is to the system.

Waterfall assumes requirements are known and stable upfront. Iterative development assumes requirements will change and delivers working software in short cycles to get feedback. For most software projects, requirements change, making iterative approaches the safer default.

You can fix at most two of scope, time, and cost. If the deadline and budget are fixed, scope must be negotiable. Attempting to fix all three forces the team to sacrifice quality, which creates technical debt that slows future development.

No single process model is universally best. The right process depends on project size, requirement stability, team experience, regulatory environment, and risk tolerance. Choosing a process model is itself a design decision.

learning_objectives: Elicit and document software requirements using user stories and acceptance criteria
Distinguish between functional and non-functional requirements
Prioritize requirements using MoSCoW and value-effort matrices
Identify common requirements elicitation pitfalls and mitigation strategies
readings: Sommerville, Ch. 4 - Requirements Engineering
Cohn, User Stories Applied, Ch. 1-4
Robertson & Robertson, Mastering the Requirements Process, Ch. 5

lecture_notes.introduction: The most expensive bugs are requirements bugs: building the wrong thing. Studies consistently show that fixing a requirements defect after deployment costs 50-200 times more than fixing it during requirements analysis. Requirements engineering is the systematic process of understanding what the customer actually needs, which is frequently different from what they initially say they want. Elicitation techniques such as interviews, workshops, observation, and prototyping are necessary because stakeholders often cannot articulate their needs in terms that translate directly into software specifications. Functional requirements describe what the system does: process a payment, send a notification, generate a report. Non-functional requirements describe how the system behaves: response time under 200ms, 99.9% availability, WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility compliance. Non-functional requirements are frequently overlooked during elicitation but dominate architectural decisions. A system that must handle 10,000 concurrent users requires a fundamentally different architecture than one serving 50. Requirements traceability, the ability to link each requirement to its implementation and tests, ensures that nothing is forgotten during development and that every line of code has a justifiable purpose. This session teaches you to extract, organize, validate, and manage requirements as living documents that evolve with the project.
key_concepts: User stories: 'As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]' format that captures who, what, and why in a concise, negotiable form
Acceptance criteria: Given/When/Then format (Gherkin syntax) that makes requirements testable and unambiguous
INVEST criteria: good user stories are Independent, Negotiable, Valuable, Estimable, Small, and Testable
Functional vs non-functional requirements: functional (what the system does) vs quality attributes (performance, security, scalability, usability, reliability)
MoSCoW prioritization: Must have, Should have, Could have, Won't have for this release — a negotiation framework between stakeholders and the development team
Requirements elicitation techniques: stakeholder interviews, workshops, observation, questionnaires, prototyping, and domain analysis
Requirements validation: reviews, walkthroughs, and prototyping to confirm requirements are correct, consistent, complete, and feasible before implementation begins
Traceability matrix: linking requirements to design elements, code modules, and test cases to ensure coverage and manage change impact
Scope creep: uncontrolled expansion of requirements without corresponding adjustments to schedule, budget, or staffing
Requirements volatility: measuring and planning for the rate at which requirements change, which varies by domain and project phase
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a vague customer request ('build me a scheduling app for a veterinary clinic') and decompose it through a live simulated stakeholder interview into 15 user stories with acceptance criteria. Demonstrate how probing questions reveal hidden non-functional requirements (appointment conflicts, HIPAA-like data privacy for pet medical records, SMS notification delivery within 30 seconds) that would otherwise surface as late-stage surprises.
setup_requirements: Project management tool configured with backlog view
User story template and acceptance criteria template provided
Sample stakeholder interview script

review.value: Good user stories are Independent (can be developed in any order), Negotiable (details are conversation starters, not contracts), Valuable (each delivers user value), Estimable (team can size the effort), Small (fits in one sprint), and Testable (has clear acceptance criteria).

Given/When/Then format makes requirements testable: Given [precondition], When [action], Then [expected result]. If you cannot write acceptance criteria for a requirement, the requirement is not sufficiently understood to implement.

Quality attributes like performance, security, and availability are often the primary drivers of architectural decisions. A system that must serve 10,000 concurrent users needs fundamentally different architecture than one serving 50 users.

Uncontrolled addition of features without adjusting schedule or resources. Managed through a change control process: every new requirement is evaluated for impact on scope, schedule, and budget before being accepted into the backlog.

learning_objectives: Create UML use case diagrams to capture system behavior from the user perspective
Design class diagrams that represent system structure with proper relationships
Model dynamic behavior using sequence diagrams and state machine diagrams
Evaluate when modeling adds value versus when it creates unnecessary overhead
readings: Sommerville, Ch. 5 - System Modeling
Fowler, UML Distilled, 3rd Ed., Ch. 3-6, 9-10
Martin, Agile Software Development, Ch. 13-14 (UML in Agile)

lecture_notes.introduction: Models are abstractions that help us reason about complex systems before building them. The Unified Modeling Language (UML) provides a standardized notation for visualizing software architecture, behavior, and interactions. However, the value of modeling lies not in the diagrams themselves but in the conversations they provoke and the design decisions they make explicit. A class diagram that sits in a wiki unread by anyone is waste; a whiteboard sketch that aligns a team on a tricky interaction is invaluable. UML diagrams serve as communication tools during design, documentation artifacts for onboarding, and analysis aids for identifying structural problems before they become code problems. Use case diagrams capture the system boundary and actor interactions from the user perspective. Class diagrams show the static structure: classes, attributes, operations, and relationships (association, aggregation, composition, inheritance, dependency). Sequence diagrams model the dynamic interaction between objects over time, making the flow of messages and method calls explicit. State machine diagrams capture the lifecycle of objects that transition between well-defined states in response to events. The key skill is knowing which diagram type to use for a given design question and how much detail is appropriate for the audience and purpose.
key_concepts: Use case diagrams: actors, use cases, system boundary, include and extend relationships for capturing functional requirements visually

Class diagrams: classes with attributes and operations, associations (multiplicity), aggregation (has-a, lifecycle independent), composition (has-a, lifecycle dependent), inheritance (is-a), and dependency
Sequence diagrams: objects as lifelines, synchronous and asynchronous messages, activation bars, return messages, and combined fragments (alt, loop, opt) for modeling method call sequences
State machine diagrams: states, transitions, events, guards, and actions for modeling object lifecycle behavior (e.g., order states: placed, paid, shipped, delivered, cancelled)
Activity diagrams: flowchart-like representation of workflows with decision nodes, fork/join for parallelism, and swimlanes for responsibility assignment
Modeling levels: conceptual (domain analysis, no implementation details), specification (interfaces and contracts), implementation (code-level detail) — match the level to the audience
Agile modeling: lightweight, just-enough modeling as a design tool rather than comprehensive upfront documentation; models are disposable conversation artifacts
Domain-driven design connection: ubiquitous language and bounded contexts expressed through UML class diagrams that become the shared vocabulary between developers and domain experts

Common modeling mistakes: over-modeling (diagramming every trivial class), under-modeling (skipping diagrams for genuinely complex interactions), and stale models (diagrams that diverge from the actual code)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Model an e-commerce order processing system: start with a use case diagram showing customer, admin, and payment gateway actors; derive a class diagram with Order, OrderItem, Product, Customer, and Payment classes showing proper relationships and multiplicities; create a sequence diagram for the checkout flow showing the interaction between UI, OrderService, PaymentGateway, and InventoryService; build a state machine for Order lifecycle (created, payment_pending, paid, fulfilling, shipped, delivered, cancelled, refunded).
setup_requirements: UML diagramming tool (draw.io, PlantUML, Lucidchart, or Mermaid)
PlantUML VS Code extension for text-based diagrams
E-commerce domain reference handout

review.value: Aggregation: the part can exist independently of the whole (a Student belongs to a University but exists without it). Composition: the part cannot exist without the whole (an OrderItem cannot exist without its Order). This distinction affects lifecycle management and cascading deletes.

Sequence diagrams are most valuable for complex interactions involving multiple objects and conditional logic. They make the message flow explicit, revealing design problems like circular dependencies or god objects that orchestrate everything.

Model when the design is genuinely complex, when multiple people need to agree on an approach, or when the cost of getting the design wrong is high. Do not model simple CRUD operations or well-understood patterns that the team already knows.

State machine diagrams are essential for any object with a non-trivial lifecycle. They make valid state transitions explicit and reveal impossible transitions that code must prevent, reducing bugs caused by objects in unexpected states.

learning_objectives: Evaluate and select architectural patterns based on system requirements
Design systems using layered, microservices, event-driven, and pipe-and-filter architectures
Analyze quality attribute trade-offs between different architectural approaches
Document architectural decisions using Architecture Decision Records
readings: Sommerville, Ch. 6 - Architectural Design
Richards & Ford, Fundamentals of Software Architecture, Ch. 9-14
Bass, Clements & Kazman, Software Architecture in Practice, Ch. 4, 13

lecture_notes.introduction: Architecture is the set of design decisions that are expensive to change. While individual lines of code can be refactored cheaply, the fundamental structure of a system — how it is decomposed into components, how those components communicate, and where data is stored — determines the system's quality attributes for its entire lifetime. A poor architecture constrains every future feature: adding a new capability to a tightly-coupled monolith may require changes in dozens of files, while the same feature in a well-designed modular system might require changes in one. The choice of architectural pattern is driven by quality attribute requirements. A system that must scale horizontally to handle millions of concurrent requests needs a different architecture than an internal tool used by twenty people. A system that must process financial transactions with strict consistency guarantees needs a different approach than a social media feed that tolerates eventual consistency. This lecture covers the major architectural patterns, the quality attributes they optimize for, and the trade-offs they impose. Architecture is not about choosing the most sophisticated pattern; it is about choosing the pattern whose trade-offs best align with the system's actual requirements.
key_concepts: Layered architecture: presentation, business logic, data access layers with strict dependency direction; simple and well-understood but can lead to unnecessary coupling through layer-spanning changes
Model-View-Controller (MVC): separating data representation (Model), user interface (View), and request handling (Controller); widely used in web frameworks

Microservices architecture: independently deployable services organized around business capabilities, communicating via APIs; enables independent scaling and deployment but introduces distributed systems complexity
Event-driven architecture: components communicate through events (publish-subscribe), enabling loose coupling and asynchronous processing; essential for real-time systems and complex workflows
Pipe-and-filter architecture: data flows through a sequence of processing stages (filters) connected by channels (pipes); ideal for data transformation pipelines and stream processing
Client-server and n-tier architectures: separating concerns across network boundaries with clear API contracts between tiers
Quality attribute trade-offs: scalability vs simplicity, consistency vs availability (CAP theorem), performance vs maintainability, coupling vs cohesion
Architecture Decision Records (ADRs): documenting the context, decision, and consequences of architectural choices so future developers understand why the system is built the way it is
Conway's Law: the structure of a software system mirrors the communication structure of the organization that builds it; organizational design and architectural design are inseparable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a ride-sharing application three ways: (1) as a monolithic layered architecture, (2) as microservices (rider service, driver service, matching service, payment service, notification service), and (3) as an event-driven system with a message broker. Compare each approach against the system's quality attribute requirements: real-time matching latency under 3 seconds, horizontal scaling during peak hours, independent deployment of payment processing for PCI compliance, and fault isolation so that a notification service failure does not prevent ride matching.
setup_requirements: Architecture diagramming tool (draw.io, Mermaid, or C4 model tools)
ADR template (Nygard format)
Quality attribute requirements checklist

review.value: Start with a monolith unless you have a specific, validated reason for microservices. Microservices add network latency, distributed transaction complexity, operational overhead, and debugging difficulty. A well-structured monolith is simpler to develop, test, and deploy.

Events decouple producers from consumers: the order service publishes 'OrderPlaced' without knowing which services react to it. This enables adding new consumers (analytics, notifications, fraud detection) without modifying the producer.

An ADR captures: the context (what situation prompted the decision), the decision (what was chosen), and the consequences (what trade-offs were accepted). Future developers can understand not just what was built, but why alternatives were rejected.

Architecture evaluation requires concrete scenarios: 'When 10,000 users simultaneously search for rides, the system returns matches within 3 seconds' is testable. 'The system should be scalable' is not. Quality attribute scenarios drive and validate architectural choices.

learning_objectives: Apply creational patterns (Factory Method, Abstract Factory, Singleton, Builder) to manage object creation complexity
Use structural patterns (Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Composite) to compose flexible class hierarchies
Implement behavioral patterns (Strategy, Observer, Command, State) to manage complex control flow
Recognize pattern applicability from problem characteristics rather than memorizing pattern catalogs
readings: Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides, Design Patterns, Ch. 1-2 and selected patterns
Freeman & Robson, Head First Design Patterns, Ch. 1-4
Fowler, 'Is Design Dead?' (article on patterns in Agile)

lecture_notes.introduction: Design patterns are proven solutions to recurring software design problems. The seminal 'Gang of Four' book (Gamma, Helm, Johnson, Vlissides, 1994) cataloged 23 patterns organized into creational, structural, and behavioral categories. Patterns are not code templates to copy-paste; they are named abstractions that capture the essence of a design solution, its applicability conditions, and its consequences. Knowing patterns gives you a shared vocabulary with other developers: saying 'use the Observer pattern here' communicates more design intent in four words than a paragraph of implementation description. However, patterns are tools, not goals. Applying a pattern where it is not needed adds unnecessary complexity. The Strategy pattern is elegant when you genuinely need interchangeable algorithms at runtime, but over-engineering a simple conditional into a Strategy hierarchy is worse than the conditional. Each pattern has a problem context (when to use it), a solution structure (how to implement it), and consequences (what trade-offs it introduces). This lecture focuses on the most practically useful patterns, teaching you to recognize the problem context that signals a pattern is appropriate. We will implement several patterns in code, seeing how they simplify complex problems and how they interact with SOLID principles and dependency injection.
key_concepts: Factory Method: define an interface for creating objects but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate; eliminates tight coupling between creator and concrete product classes
Abstract Factory: create families of related objects without specifying concrete classes; useful when a system must work with multiple product variants (e.g., UI toolkit for different platforms)

Builder: separate the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing the same construction process to create different representations; prevents constructors with dozens of parameters
Strategy: define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable at runtime; replaces conditional logic with polymorphism
Observer: define a one-to-many dependency so that when one object changes state, all dependents are notified automatically; the foundation of event systems and reactive programming
Decorator: attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically without modifying its class; an alternative to subclassing that supports composable behavior (e.g., Java I/O streams)
Adapter: convert the interface of a class into another interface clients expect; enables integration of incompatible third-party libraries without modifying their source code
Command: encapsulate a request as an object, enabling parameterization of clients, queuing of requests, and undo/redo functionality
SOLID principles as pattern foundations: Single Responsibility, Open/Closed, Liskov Substitution, Interface Segregation, Dependency Inversion form the principles that patterns operationalize

Anti-patterns: recognizing when pattern application adds complexity without solving a real problem (pattern fever), and understanding common anti-patterns like God Object, Spaghetti Code, and Golden Hammer
lecture_notes.demonstration: Refactor a tightly-coupled payment processing system: (1) Extract a PaymentStrategy interface with CreditCardPayment, PayPalPayment, and CryptoPayment implementations using the Strategy pattern. (2) Add logging and retry behavior using the Decorator pattern wrapping the payment strategies. (3) Implement an Observer to notify order service, analytics, and email service when payments complete. Show how each refactoring step improves testability by enabling mock injection.
setup_requirements: IDE with refactoring support (IntelliJ, VS Code)
Design pattern reference card (GoF pattern catalog summary)
Sample payment processing codebase with tight coupling

review.value: Use Strategy when new algorithm variants are likely (open/closed principle). Use a simple conditional when the set of alternatives is stable and small. Over-applying Strategy to a two-branch if statement adds complexity without benefit.

Decouples the subject (event source) from observers (event handlers). The subject does not know what observers do, only that they implement an update interface. This enables adding new reactions to events without modifying the event source.

Inheritance creates rigid hierarchies (a LoggingRetryingCreditCardPayment class for every combination). Decorators compose behaviors dynamically: wrap any payment in a LoggingDecorator, then a RetryDecorator, in any order.

Recognize patterns by the problem, not by the solution. If you have a family of interchangeable algorithms, think Strategy. If you need to notify multiple objects of state changes, think Observer. If you need to construct complex objects step by step, think Builder.

Design patterns are concrete applications of SOLID principles. Strategy implements Open/Closed. Observer implements Dependency Inversion. Factory Method implements the Liskov Substitution Principle by returning subtypes through a common interface.

learning_objectives: Establish and enforce coding standards across a development team
Write clean, self-documenting code that minimizes the need for comments
Apply naming conventions, code organization, and documentation practices that scale to large codebases
Configure automated formatting and linting tools to enforce standards consistently
readings: Martin, Clean Code, Ch. 1-4 (Clean Code, Meaningful Names, Functions, Comments)
Google Style Guides (Java or Python) - selected sections
McConnell, Code Complete, Ch. 11 (Naming) and Ch. 31 (Layout and Style)

lecture_notes.introduction: Code is read far more often than it is written. Over a typical software project's lifetime, developers spend roughly 10 times more time reading existing code than writing new code. This makes readability the single most important property of production code. Coding standards exist not to impose arbitrary aesthetic preferences but to reduce cognitive load: when every file in a project follows the same naming conventions, indentation, and organizational patterns, developers can focus on understanding logic rather than deciphering style. Self-documenting code uses meaningful names, small focused functions, and clear control flow to communicate intent directly. Comments should explain why, not what — the code itself should make the what obvious. A function named calculateMonthlyInterest is self-documenting; a function named calc needs a comment. Automated tools (formatters, linters, static analyzers) enforce standards consistently and remove style debates from code reviews, freeing reviewers to focus on logic and design. This session covers practical standards for naming, function design, code organization, error handling, and documentation, along with the tooling to enforce them automatically.
key_concepts: Naming conventions: classes are PascalCase nouns, methods are camelCase verbs, constants are UPPER_SNAKE_CASE, booleans start with is/has/can/should
Function design: small (under 20 lines), single responsibility, descriptive name, minimal parameters (ideally 0-2, maximum 3), no side effects beyond the stated purpose
Code organization: group by feature (not by type), consistent file structure within each module, clear public API surface with private implementation details hidden
Comment discipline: explain why (business rules, non-obvious decisions), not what (the code shows what); delete commented-out code (version control preserves history)
Error handling: use exceptions for exceptional conditions, return values for expected outcomes; never swallow exceptions silently; include context in error messages
Linting and formatting: ESLint, Pylint, RuboCop, dart analyze — configure once and enforce via pre-commit hooks so style violations never reach code review
Static analysis: tools like SonarQube, CodeClimate, and language-specific analyzers that detect bugs, security vulnerabilities, and complexity issues automatically
Code complexity metrics: cyclomatic complexity (number of independent paths through a function), cognitive complexity (how hard the code is for a human to understand)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a 150-line function with poor naming, deep nesting, magic numbers, commented-out code, and swallowed exceptions. Refactor it live into clean code: extract well-named helper functions, replace magic numbers with named constants, convert nested conditionals to early returns (guard clauses), add proper error handling with contextual messages, and configure a linter to prevent regression.
setup_requirements: IDE with linting and formatting configured
Pre-commit hook framework (Husky for JS, pre-commit for Python)
Sample codebase with intentional style violations

review.value: A function named getUsersByActiveSubscription(planType) needs no comment. A function named get(type) needs a paragraph. Invest in names, not comments. The best comment is the one you did not need to write because the code was clear.

Replace deeply nested conditionals with early returns at the top of the function. Each guard clause handles one invalid case and exits immediately, leaving the happy path at the base indentation level.

Style rules enforced by humans in code review create friction and inconsistency. Style rules enforced by automated tools (formatters, linters, pre-commit hooks) are consistent, fast, and remove style debates from code review entirely.

A function with cyclomatic complexity over 10 has too many independent paths to test and reason about reliably. Refactor by extracting helper functions, each handling one specific case with its own unit test.

learning_objectives: Use Git branching strategies effectively for team collaboration
Configure continuous integration pipelines with automated build, lint, test, and deploy stages
Implement continuous deployment with feature flags and canary releases
Design release management workflows that balance velocity with stability
readings: Chacon & Straub, Pro Git, Ch. 3 (Git Branching) and Ch. 5 (Distributed Git)
Humble & Farley, Continuous Delivery, Ch. 1-3, 5
Forsgren, Humble & Kim, Accelerate, Ch. 4 (Technical Practices)

lecture_notes.introduction: Version control and CI/CD are non-negotiable practices in professional software development. They solve problems that every team encounters at scale: how do multiple developers work on the same codebase without stepping on each other's changes, how do we ensure that every code change is tested before it reaches users, and how do we deploy software frequently and reliably without manual intervention? Git is the universal version control system, but the branching strategy a team adopts determines whether Git helps or hinders their workflow. Git Flow, GitHub Flow, and trunk-based development represent different trade-offs between isolation (long-lived branches reduce risk of breaking main but create merge conflicts) and integration speed (short-lived branches merge frequently, catching integration issues early). Continuous Integration means every developer pushes to a shared branch at least daily, and each push triggers an automated build-and-test pipeline. Continuous Deployment extends CI by automatically deploying every passing build to production. Feature flags decouple deployment from release: code is deployed to production but hidden behind a flag that is enabled separately, allowing gradual rollout, A/B testing, and instant rollback without a new deployment.
key_concepts: Git branching strategies: Git Flow (long-lived develop and release branches), GitHub Flow (short-lived feature branches off main), trunk-based development (everyone commits to main with feature flags)
Pull request workflow: create branch, make changes, push, open PR, automated checks run, human review, approve, merge, delete branch
Merge strategies: merge commit (preserves history), squash merge (clean history, loses granularity), rebase (linear history, rewrites commits)
CI pipeline stages: checkout code, install dependencies, lint/format check, unit tests, integration tests, build artifact, report results
CD pipeline: CI succeeds, deploy to staging automatically, run smoke tests, deploy to production (manually gated or automatically)

Feature flags: wrapping new features in runtime configuration toggles, enabling deployment without exposure; managed through feature flag services (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, or simple environment variables)
Canary releases: deploying to a small percentage of production traffic first, monitoring error rates and latency, then gradually increasing to full deployment
Infrastructure as Code: defining CI/CD pipelines, deployment environments, and infrastructure in version-controlled configuration files (GitHub Actions YAML, Terraform, Docker Compose)
Build reproducibility: pinning dependency versions, using lockfiles, containerizing builds so that the same commit always produces the same artifact
lecture_notes.demonstration: Set up a complete GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline from scratch: (1) create a workflow file that triggers on pull requests and pushes to main, (2) add lint, unit test, and integration test jobs running in parallel, (3) add a build step that produces a Docker image, (4) deploy to a staging environment on merge to main, (5) add a manual approval gate for production deployment. Show how a failing test blocks the PR merge and how a feature flag allows deploying code to production while keeping the feature hidden.
setup_requirements: GitHub repository with GitHub Actions enabled
Sample project with existing test suite and Dockerfile
Docker installed for containerized testing
Feature flag library configured (e.g., unleash-client or environment variables)

review.value: All developers commit to main with short-lived feature branches (ideally less than a day). This minimizes merge conflicts and integration risk. Feature flags handle incomplete features so main is always deployable.

Wrapping new features in configuration flags decouples deployment from release. Code goes to production behind a disabled flag, is enabled gradually (canary), and can be instantly disabled if problems are discovered — without a new deployment.

CI/CD configuration lives in the repository alongside the application code (e.g., .github/workflows/). This means pipeline changes go through the same PR review process as application changes, and every commit has a corresponding pipeline definition.

Elite teams deploy multiple times per day with lead times under an hour (DORA metrics). This is only possible with automated testing, automated deployment, and feature flags. Manual processes create bottlenecks that reduce deployment frequency.

learning_objectives: Design a comprehensive test strategy covering unit, integration, and end-to-end testing
Write effective unit tests with proper isolation using mocks, stubs, and fakes
Understand the testing pyramid and invest test effort proportionally at each level
Evaluate test quality metrics beyond code coverage
readings: Sommerville, Ch. 8 - Software Testing
Meszaros, xUnit Test Patterns, Ch. 1-4 and Ch. 11
Fowler, 'TestPyramid' and 'UnitTest' (martinfowler.com articles)

lecture_notes.introduction: Testing is not optional in professional software development. The question is not whether to test, but how much of each kind of testing provides the optimal balance of confidence, speed, and maintainability. The testing pyramid, introduced by Mike Cohn, provides the framework: a wide base of many fast unit tests, a middle layer of fewer integration tests, and a narrow top of even fewer end-to-end tests. This shape reflects the trade-offs at each level. Unit tests are fast (milliseconds), isolated (test one unit of behavior), and cheap to write and maintain, but they cannot catch integration bugs. Integration tests verify that components work together correctly — that the service layer actually communicates with the database correctly, that the API endpoint actually calls the service with the right parameters — but they are slower (seconds) and more complex to set up. End-to-end tests verify the entire system from the user's perspective, catching bugs that span multiple integration boundaries, but they are slow (minutes), brittle (break when any component changes), and expensive to maintain. Inverting the pyramid — relying primarily on E2E tests — leads to slow, unreliable test suites that developers stop trusting and stop running. Each test level catches defects that the others miss, and a balanced strategy invests effort at each level proportionally to the return on confidence it provides.
key_concepts: Testing pyramid: many unit tests (fast, isolated, cheap), fewer integration tests (slower, test boundaries), few E2E tests (slowest, most brittle, highest confidence per test)
Unit tests: test one function or method in isolation from its dependencies; should run in milliseconds; use test doubles to replace external dependencies
Integration tests: test the interaction between two or more components (service + database, API + service); verify that interfaces and contracts work correctly at boundaries
End-to-end tests: test the entire system from the user's perspective (browser clicks button, API processes request, database stores data, UI shows confirmation)

Test doubles: stub (returns canned answers), mock (verifies interactions were called with expected arguments), fake (working lightweight implementation like in-memory database), spy (records calls for later assertion)
Test-Driven Development preview: write the test first (red), write minimal code to pass (green), refactor while keeping tests passing; TDD is covered in depth next week
Test quality metrics: mutation testing (injecting faults to verify tests catch them), assertion density (tests with no assertions provide no value), test execution speed, flakiness rate

Code coverage: measures which lines/branches are executed by tests; 100% coverage does not mean no bugs (a covered line with no assertion is tested in name only); useful as a lower bound, not a target
lecture_notes.demonstration: Write unit tests with mocked dependencies for an OrderService (mocking PaymentGateway and InventoryService). Then write integration tests that use a real test database to verify OrderRepository behavior. Compare execution speed: unit tests complete in 50ms, integration tests in 2 seconds. Demonstrate mutation testing with a tool (Stryker or mutmut) showing that a test suite with 95% line coverage misses injected faults because assertions are too weak.
setup_requirements: Testing framework configured (pytest, JUnit, Jest, or similar)
Mocking library (unittest.mock, Mockito, or jest.fn)
Sample service with database dependency
Mutation testing tool (Stryker, mutmut, or PITest)

review.value: Many unit tests (fast, isolated, cheap), some integration tests (slower, test component boundaries), few E2E tests (slow, brittle, expensive). Inverting the pyramid leads to slow CI pipelines and flaky tests that developers ignore.

Stub: returns canned answers for the test scenario. Mock: verifies that specific methods were called with expected arguments. Fake: a working lightweight implementation (e.g., in-memory database). Spy: a real object that records interactions for later assertion.

Mutation testing changes production code (mutates operators, deletes statements, changes return values) and checks if tests fail. Surviving mutants indicate weak tests. This is a more meaningful quality metric than line coverage.

Each test must be independent: it sets up its own state, exercises the code, and verifies the result without relying on other tests having run first. Shared mutable state between tests causes flakiness and order-dependent failures.

learning_objectives: Practice the TDD cycle: red (write a failing test), green (write minimal code to pass), refactor (improve design while tests pass)
Use TDD to drive design decisions and discover interfaces organically
Apply TDD to both new feature development and bug fixing (regression-first development)
Evaluate when TDD provides the highest return on investment and when it may not be appropriate
readings: Beck, Test-Driven Development By Example, Ch. 1-17
Astels, Test-Driven Development: A Practical Guide, Ch. 3-5
Martin, Clean Code, Ch. 9 (Unit Tests)

lecture_notes.introduction: Test-Driven Development inverts the conventional development workflow. Instead of writing code and then writing tests to verify it, TDD practitioners write the test first, watch it fail, then write the minimum code necessary to make it pass. This inversion has profound consequences for software design. Because you write the test before the implementation, you are forced to think about the interface (how the code will be called) before the implementation (how it works internally). This produces code with cleaner interfaces, fewer unnecessary features, and built-in test coverage. The TDD cycle is deceptively simple: Red (write a test that fails because the code does not exist yet), Green (write the simplest possible code to make the test pass, even if it is ugly), Refactor (improve the code structure while all tests remain green). The discipline of the Green step is critical: resist the urge to write the 'correct' implementation immediately. Writing the simplest passing code forces you to add complexity only when a new test demands it, preventing over-engineering. The Refactor step is where design patterns emerge organically: when you see duplication, you extract it. When you see a function doing too much, you split it. The tests protect you during refactoring, giving you confidence that behavior is preserved.
key_concepts: Red-Green-Refactor cycle: (1) write a failing test that defines desired behavior, (2) write the simplest code that makes the test pass, (3) refactor to improve design while keeping all tests green
Test-first design: writing the test before the code forces you to define the interface first, producing code that is inherently testable and has minimal unnecessary API surface
Triangulation: when the simplest passing implementation is a hardcoded return value, write a second test with different input to force generalization of the implementation
Transformation Priority Premise: a ranked list of code transformations (constant → variable → conditional → iteration → recursion) that guides the Green step from simple to general
Regression-first development: when fixing a bug, write a test that reproduces the bug first (red), then fix the code (green); the test prevents the bug from recurring
TDD and design emergence: design patterns and abstractions emerge from the refactoring step rather than being planned upfront; the code tells you what abstractions it needs
Outside-in TDD (London school): start with an acceptance test for the feature, then TDD the inner components, using mocks for dependencies not yet implemented
Inside-out TDD (Detroit school): start with the innermost domain logic, TDD each layer, then compose them; less mocking, more focus on state verification
When TDD is most valuable: complex business logic, algorithmic code, code with many edge cases, critical-path features; less valuable for simple CRUD, UI layout, and exploratory prototyping
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live-code a string calculator kata using strict TDD: (1) empty string returns 0 (write test, fail, implement), (2) single number returns itself, (3) two comma-separated numbers return sum, (4) handle arbitrary count of numbers, (5) support newline delimiter, (6) support custom delimiter syntax, (7) negative numbers throw an exception listing all negatives. Show how each test drives one small increment of functionality and how the refactoring step extracts parsing logic into a helper function when duplication appears.
setup_requirements: Testing framework with fast test runner (pytest with --watch, Jest with --watch, or similar)
IDE with test runner integration for instant feedback
Kata starting template (empty project with test framework configured)

review.value: Write the simplest code that passes the test, even if it looks wrong (e.g., returning a hardcoded value). The next test will force you to generalize. This discipline prevents over-engineering and ensures every line of code is justified by a test.

When fixing a bug, always write a failing test that reproduces the bug before writing the fix. This ensures (1) you understand the bug precisely, (2) the fix actually works, and (3) the bug cannot recur without breaking the test.

TDD enables fearless refactoring: comprehensive test coverage means you can restructure code with confidence that behavior is preserved. Without tests, refactoring is risky and developers avoid it, allowing code to rot.

TDD has overhead. For exploratory prototyping, throwaway scripts, and simple CRUD with no business logic, the cost of test-first development may exceed the benefit. Use judgment — TDD is a tool, not a religion.

learning_objectives: Conduct effective code reviews that improve quality without demoralizing authors
Identify code smells and apply systematic refactoring techniques
Use automated tools to catch common issues before human review
Build a code review culture that balances velocity with quality
readings: Fowler, Refactoring: Improving the Design of Existing Code, Ch. 1-3
Google Engineering Practices: Code Review Guide
Ousterhout, A Philosophy of Software Design, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: Code quality is not about aesthetics. It directly affects defect rates, development speed, and team morale. Studies at Google and Microsoft have shown that code review catches 60-70% of defects before they reach testing, making it one of the most cost-effective quality practices available. But code review is only effective when done well. Large pull requests get rubber-stamped because reviewers cannot maintain attention through 500 lines of changes. Adversarial reviews that focus on nitpicking style create friction and discourage contribution. Effective code review requires small, focused pull requests (ideally under 200 lines), constructive feedback that explains the reasoning behind suggestions, and a culture where review comments are received as gifts rather than attacks. Refactoring complements code review: when a reviewer identifies a structural problem, the author refactors to address it. Refactoring is the systematic improvement of code structure without changing its external behavior. Martin Fowler's catalog identifies dozens of named refactorings (Extract Method, Extract Class, Rename, Move Method, Inline) that can be applied mechanically and safely when the code is covered by tests. Static analysis tools automate the detection of many code smells, freeing human reviewers to focus on design, logic, and business rule correctness.
key_concepts: Code smells: long methods (>20 lines), god classes (>300 lines, multiple responsibilities), feature envy (method uses another class's data more than its own), duplicated code, primitive obsession, data clumps
Refactoring catalog: Extract Method, Extract Class, Rename, Move Method, Inline Method, Replace Conditional with Polymorphism, Introduce Parameter Object, Replace Magic Number with Named Constant
Refactoring safety: always refactor under test coverage; each step should keep all tests green; if a test breaks, the refactoring introduced a behavioral change (a bug)

Code review best practices: review PRs under 200 lines, focus comments on logic and design (not style — automate that), explain the 'why' behind suggestions, approve with minor comments rather than blocking

Review comment types: must-fix (blocking bugs, security issues), suggestion (design improvements), nit (style preferences that should be automated), question (seeking understanding, not demanding change)
Static analysis in review: configure tools to run automatically on PRs and report findings as inline comments, reducing the reviewer's burden for mechanical checks
Refactoring vs rewriting: refactoring preserves behavior while improving structure (small, safe steps); rewriting replaces code entirely (risky, often loses hidden bug fixes embedded in the old code)
Code review metrics: time to first review, review turnaround time, PR size distribution, defect detection rate — track these to improve the review process
lecture_notes.demonstration: Take a 200-line function with multiple code smells (long method, deep nesting, duplicated logic, magic numbers, feature envy). Refactor it live in small steps: (1) extract a validation helper, (2) replace magic numbers with named constants, (3) convert nested conditionals to guard clauses, (4) extract a calculation method that belongs on another class (moving it reduces feature envy), (5) rename variables for clarity. Verify all tests pass after each step. Then conduct a mock code review of a classmate's pull request, demonstrating constructive feedback.
setup_requirements: IDE with refactoring shortcuts configured (Shift+F6 rename, Ctrl+Alt+M extract method)
Linter and static analysis tool running on the sample codebase
Sample codebase with existing tests and intentional code smells
GitHub PR with review interface

review.value: Never refactor without tests covering the code being changed. Each refactoring step should be a single change that keeps all tests green. If a test breaks, the refactoring introduced a bug. Undo and try a smaller step.

Code review effectiveness drops sharply for large PRs. Reviewers skim 500-line PRs but carefully read 50-line PRs. Split large changes into a series of small, focused PRs that each address one concern.

Frame suggestions as questions ('Have you considered X?') or explanations ('If we extract this, testing becomes easier because...'). Avoid commands ('Change this') and personal criticism ('You always do this wrong').

Style, formatting, import order, and simple pattern violations should be caught by automated tools, not human reviewers. Every style comment in a code review represents a failure to configure the linter.

learning_objectives: Plan and execute Scrum sprints with proper ceremonies (planning, daily standup, review, retrospective)
Estimate work using story points, planning poker, and velocity tracking
Manage a product backlog with prioritization, refinement, and acceptance criteria
Identify and address common Agile anti-patterns that undermine team effectiveness
readings: Schwaber & Sutherland, The Scrum Guide (2020 edition)
Cohn, Agile Estimating and Planning, Ch. 1-6
Forsgren, Humble & Kim, Accelerate, Ch. 1-3 (Measuring and Improving Delivery)

lecture_notes.introduction: Agile software development is built on a simple premise: in most software projects, requirements change, and processes that embrace change outperform processes that resist it. The Agile Manifesto (2001) distilled this insight into four values and twelve principles that have transformed the software industry. Scrum, the most widely adopted Agile framework, provides a structured approach: fixed-length sprints (typically 2 weeks), defined roles (Product Owner who prioritizes the backlog, Scrum Master who facilitates the process, Development Team who delivers the work), and ceremonies (Sprint Planning where the team commits to sprint goals, Daily Standup for synchronization, Sprint Review for stakeholder feedback, Sprint Retrospective for process improvement). However, many organizations adopt Scrum mechanically without understanding its principles, creating 'cargo cult Agile' that has the rituals but not the benefits. Common anti-patterns include sprint planning without a refined backlog (leading to vague commitments), daily standups that become status reports to managers rather than team synchronization, sprint reviews without stakeholder feedback, and retrospectives without follow-through on action items. Effective Agile requires not just following the ceremonies but embodying the values: responding to change, delivering working software frequently, building projects around motivated individuals, and reflecting regularly on how to become more effective.
key_concepts: Scrum roles: Product Owner (maximizes value by prioritizing the backlog), Scrum Master (facilitates the process and removes impediments), Development Team (self-organizing cross-functional group that delivers the increment)

Sprint ceremonies: Sprint Planning (what can we deliver and how?), Daily Standup (what did I do, what will I do, what blocks me? — 15 minutes max), Sprint Review (demonstrate working software to stakeholders), Sprint Retrospective (what went well, what did not, what will we change?)
Product backlog management: user stories ordered by value, INVEST criteria for story quality, regular refinement sessions to split and estimate upcoming stories
Estimation with story points: relative sizing (this story is twice as complex as that one), planning poker for team consensus, velocity (average points completed per sprint) for capacity planning
Definition of Done: a shared checklist that every story must satisfy (code reviewed, tests passing, documentation updated, deployed to staging) to prevent partially complete work from accumulating
Agile metrics: velocity (capacity), lead time (idea to production), cycle time (work started to work done), sprint burndown (remaining work over time), cumulative flow diagram
Kanban principles: visualize work, limit work-in-progress (WIP), manage flow, make process policies explicit; Kanban can complement or replace Scrum sprints

Common anti-patterns: sprinting without a refined backlog, story point inflation over time, retrospectives without action items, Scrum Master as project manager, skipping reviews because 'nothing is ready to show'
Scaling Agile: when multiple teams work on the same product, frameworks like SAFe, LeSS, and Spotify model provide coordination mechanisms, but they add overhead and should be adopted carefully
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulate a complete sprint cycle: (1) Refine a product backlog of 20 user stories for a task management application, splitting stories that are too large and adding acceptance criteria. (2) Conduct sprint planning: estimate stories using planning poker, select a sprint goal, and commit to a sprint backlog. (3) Run a simulated daily standup with three team members reporting blockers. (4) Conduct a sprint review presenting the completed increment. (5) Run a retrospective using the Start-Stop-Continue format, producing three concrete action items.
setup_requirements: Project management tool (GitHub Projects, Jira, or Linear) configured with sprint board
Planning poker cards or online tool (planningpoker.com)
Task management app backlog with 20 pre-written user stories

review.value: Velocity measures what the team has historically delivered, not what they should deliver. Using velocity as a productivity target incentivizes story point inflation and discourages honest estimation. Velocity is for capacity planning, not performance evaluation.

Without a clear, shared Definition of Done, teams accumulate 'almost done' work that is not truly releasable. Every story must meet the DoD before it can be counted as complete: code reviewed, tests passing, documentation updated, deployed to staging.

A retrospective without action items is just venting. Each retrospective should produce 1-3 specific, actionable improvements that the team commits to trying in the next sprint. Track follow-through on previous action items.

Limiting work-in-progress forces the team to finish work before starting new work. High WIP means frequent context-switching, longer cycle times, and more partially-done work. Lower WIP means faster delivery of each individual item.

learning_objectives: Understand the DevOps philosophy and its impact on software delivery
Implement infrastructure as code using Docker and container orchestration
Design monitoring and observability systems for production applications
Apply the DORA metrics framework to measure and improve delivery performance
readings: Kim, Humble, Debois & Willis, The DevOps Handbook, Ch. 1-5
Forsgren, Humble & Kim, Accelerate, Ch. 2, 4 (Capabilities and Technical Practices)
Docker Documentation: Getting Started (official docs)

lecture_notes.introduction: DevOps emerged from the recognition that the traditional wall between development teams (who write code and want to ship features fast) and operations teams (who run production and want stability) creates a dysfunctional incentive structure. Developers throw code over the wall; operations scrambles to deploy it; when production breaks, each side blames the other. DevOps dissolves this wall by making development teams responsible for the operational health of their code, using automation to make deployments safe and frequent, and treating infrastructure as code that is version-controlled, reviewed, and tested like application code. The DORA (DevOps Research and Assessment) metrics provide an empirical framework for measuring delivery performance: deployment frequency (how often code reaches production), lead time for changes (time from commit to production), change failure rate (percentage of deployments that cause incidents), and time to restore service (how quickly incidents are resolved). Elite-performing teams deploy multiple times per day with lead times under an hour, while low performers deploy monthly with lead times measured in months. The practices that enable elite performance are not secrets: automated testing, continuous integration, trunk-based development, infrastructure as code, monitoring and alerting, and a culture of shared responsibility. This session covers the technical practices and cultural principles that make DevOps work.
key_concepts: DevOps culture: shared ownership of code and production, blameless post-mortems, learning from failure, breaking down silos between development and operations
Infrastructure as Code (IaC): defining servers, networks, databases, and deployment pipelines in version-controlled configuration files (Terraform, CloudFormation, Docker Compose)
Containerization with Docker: packaging applications with all dependencies into portable, reproducible containers that run identically in development, CI, and production
Container orchestration: Kubernetes fundamentals — pods, services, deployments, scaling, health checks, rolling updates
Monitoring and observability: the three pillars — metrics (numerical measurements over time), logs (discrete events with context), traces (request flow through distributed components)
Alerting: alert on symptoms (error rate, latency), not causes; page on user-facing impact, not internal metrics; avoid alert fatigue by tuning thresholds and suppressing noise
DORA metrics: deployment frequency, lead time for changes, change failure rate, mean time to restore — the four metrics that predict software delivery performance and organizational outcomes
Incident management: detection (monitoring), response (on-call), mitigation (rollback or fix-forward), resolution (root cause analysis), prevention (blameless post-mortem with action items)

GitOps: using Git as the single source of truth for both application code and infrastructure; changes to infrastructure are made via pull requests and automatically applied by a reconciliation controller
lecture_notes.demonstration: Containerize a web application: (1) Write a multi-stage Dockerfile that builds the application in one stage and creates a minimal production image in the second. (2) Create a docker-compose.yml that runs the application, a PostgreSQL database, and a Redis cache. (3) Add a health check endpoint and configure Docker health checks. (4) Set up Prometheus for metrics collection and Grafana for visualization. (5) Simulate a deployment failure and demonstrate rollback using Docker Compose.
setup_requirements: Docker and Docker Compose installed
Sample web application with health check endpoint
Prometheus and Grafana Docker images
kubectl configured with a local Kubernetes cluster (minikube or kind) — optional

review.value: Containers share the host OS kernel (lightweight, start in seconds, minimal overhead). VMs include a full guest OS (heavier, start in minutes, stronger isolation). Containers are preferred for application deployment; VMs for strong security isolation between tenants.

The four metrics — deployment frequency, lead time, change failure rate, time to restore — are the best predictors of software delivery performance. Improving these metrics correlates with improved organizational performance and employee satisfaction.

When incidents occur, focus on what happened, why the system allowed it, and how to prevent recurrence — not who caused it. Blame discourages honesty and hides systemic issues. A blameless culture accelerates learning from failure.

Monitoring tells you when something is wrong (predefined checks). Observability lets you ask arbitrary questions about system behavior (using metrics, logs, and traces). Modern distributed systems need observability because you cannot predict what will break.

learning_objectives: Classify maintenance activities: corrective, adaptive, perfective, and preventive
Apply Lehman's laws of software evolution to predict maintenance challenges
Develop strategies for managing legacy code that lacks tests and documentation
Plan and execute incremental modernization of aging systems
readings: Sommerville, Ch. 9 - Software Evolution
Feathers, Working Effectively with Legacy Code, Ch. 1-4
Lehman, 'Programs, Life Cycles, and Laws of Software Evolution' (1980 paper)

lecture_notes.introduction: Software maintenance consumes 60-80% of total software lifecycle costs, yet it receives a fraction of the attention given to initial development in most computer science curricula. Once a system is deployed, it enters a maintenance phase that may last decades. During this phase, the software must be corrected (fixing bugs discovered in production), adapted (updating for new operating systems, browsers, APIs, and regulations), perfected (adding features and improving performance based on user feedback), and prevented from degrading (refactoring to manage increasing complexity). Lehman's laws of software evolution, derived from empirical study of large software systems, describe the forces that shape long-lived software: continuing change (a system must adapt or become progressively less satisfactory), increasing complexity (as a system evolves, its complexity increases unless work is done to reduce it), and conservation of organizational stability (the rate of development is approximately constant and independent of resources allocated). These laws explain why adding more developers to a late project makes it later (Brooks's Law) and why legacy systems that receive no investment in structural improvement become progressively harder and riskier to change. Working with legacy code — code without tests, with unclear dependencies, and with tribal knowledge that left with former developers — is a core skill for professional software engineers, because most software engineering work is maintenance, not greenfield development.
key_concepts: Maintenance categories: corrective (fixing bugs), adaptive (updating for environment changes), perfective (enhancing features and performance), preventive (refactoring to reduce future maintenance cost)
Lehman's laws: continuing change, increasing complexity, conservation of organizational stability, conservation of familiarity, continuing growth, declining quality, feedback system

Technical debt management: identifying debt (code smells, outdated dependencies, missing tests), quantifying it (estimated remediation time), prioritizing repayment (based on the code's change frequency and criticality)

Legacy code strategies: Michael Feathers' approach — find seams (points where behavior can be altered without editing), create characterization tests (tests that document current behavior, whether correct or not), then refactor safely

Strangler Fig pattern: incrementally replacing a legacy system by routing new functionality to a new system while the old system continues handling existing functionality; over time, the new system replaces the old one
Database migration strategies: expand-and-contract pattern (add new column, migrate data, update code to use new column, drop old column) for zero-downtime schema changes
Dependency management: keeping dependencies updated to avoid accumulating security vulnerabilities and compatibility issues; tools like Dependabot and Renovate automate this process
Documentation for maintenance: architecture decision records, runbooks for common operations, onboarding guides for new developers — documentation that enables others to maintain the system
Reverse engineering: understanding a legacy system's behavior through code reading, debugging, log analysis, and database schema analysis when documentation is absent or unreliable
lecture_notes.demonstration: Work with a legacy Python application (500 lines, no tests, unclear dependencies): (1) Run it and observe behavior to create a mental model. (2) Identify seams where behavior can be intercepted. (3) Write characterization tests for the three most critical code paths. (4) Use the characterization tests as a safety net to extract a tangled function into two clean functions. (5) Introduce dependency injection to make a database call testable. Show how each step incrementally improves the code's maintainability without a risky full rewrite.
setup_requirements: Legacy codebase sample (provided, 500 lines, no tests)
Testing framework for writing characterization tests
IDE with debugging support for stepping through unfamiliar code
Dependency analysis tool (pydeps, jdepend, or similar)

review.value: A characterization test documents what the code actually does, not what it should do. Write a test, run it, and use the actual output as the expected value. These tests create a safety net for refactoring even when the original intent is unclear.

Instead of a risky big-bang rewrite, incrementally replace legacy functionality. Route new requests to the new system while the old system handles existing traffic. Over time, the old system has fewer responsibilities until it can be decommissioned.

As a system evolves, its complexity increases unless deliberate work is done to reduce it. This means every sprint should include some maintenance work (refactoring, updating dependencies, improving tests) alongside feature development.

60-80% of total software cost is maintenance. Every design decision during initial development either increases or decreases future maintenance cost. Clean architecture, comprehensive tests, and clear documentation are investments in lower maintenance cost.

class_schedule.topic: Ethics and Professionalism in Software Engineering
learning_objectives: Apply the ACM/IEEE Software Engineering Code of Ethics to real-world scenarios
Identify ethical implications of software design decisions including bias, privacy, and safety
Evaluate professional responsibility when asked to ship software known to be defective or harmful
Analyze case studies of ethical failures in software engineering and their consequences
readings: ACM/IEEE Software Engineering Code of Ethics and Professional Practice
Sommerville, Ch. 1.3 (Professional Responsibility and Ethics)
O'Neil, Weapons of Math Destruction, Ch. 1-3 (selected)

lecture_notes.introduction: Software engineers build systems that affect millions of lives. Autonomous vehicles make life-and-death decisions. Recommendation algorithms shape what billions of people see and believe. Healthcare software determines treatment plans. Financial algorithms execute trades worth billions. The software engineering profession has not yet achieved the level of professional accountability found in civil engineering (where bridges that collapse lead to professional license revocation) or medicine (where malpractice has legal consequences), but the impact of software failures can be equally severe. The Therac-25 radiation therapy machine killed patients due to a race condition and inadequate testing. The Boeing 737 MAX MCAS system contributed to two crashes killing 346 people, partly due to inadequate software design review and insufficient pilot training about software behavior. Facebook's algorithm amplified divisive content to maximize engagement, contributing to real-world violence in Myanmar and elsewhere. These are not edge cases; they are the predictable consequences of prioritizing speed over safety, profit over user welfare, and technical cleverness over human impact. The ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics provides a framework for professional responsibility: act in the public interest, maintain integrity and independence of professional judgment, and ensure that the products you build meet the highest professional standards. This is not abstract philosophy; it is practical guidance for the decisions you will face in your career.
key_concepts: ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics: eight principles covering public interest, client and employer obligations, product quality, professional judgment, management, professional development, collegial behavior, and self-improvement

Algorithmic bias: training data that reflects historical discrimination produces models that perpetuate and amplify that discrimination (e.g., hiring algorithms, criminal justice risk scores, loan approval systems)

Privacy by design: building privacy protections into the architecture from the beginning (data minimization, purpose limitation, encryption at rest and in transit) rather than bolting them on after deployment

Safety-critical software: systems where failure can cause physical harm (medical devices, automotive software, industrial control) require formal verification, extensive testing, and regulatory compliance

Dark patterns: user interface designs that trick users into actions they did not intend (e.g., making unsubscribe buttons hard to find, pre-checking consent boxes, confirmshaming); these are unethical regardless of legality
Whistleblowing: the ethical obligation to report dangerous or illegal practices when internal channels fail, and the legal protections (and practical risks) for software engineers who do so
Professional responsibility: the obligation to refuse to ship software known to be dangerous, to advocate for testing and quality, and to be honest about risks even when honesty is uncomfortable
Intellectual property: understanding copyright, licensing (MIT, GPL, Apache), patents, and the ethical and legal obligations when using open-source software in commercial products
Environmental impact: the energy cost of computation (data centers, cryptocurrency mining, model training) and the professional responsibility to consider efficiency and sustainability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyze three case studies: (1) Volkswagen emissions scandal — software engineers wrote code that detected when the car was being emissions-tested and changed engine behavior to pass, while emitting 40x the legal limit during normal driving. Who was responsible? (2) Amazon's hiring algorithm that penalized resumes containing the word 'women's' because the training data reflected historical hiring bias. How should the team have responded when they discovered the bias? (3) A startup asks you to ship a medical appointment scheduling system without proper input validation because 'we need to launch next week.' What are your ethical obligations?
setup_requirements: ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics printed or digital copy
Case study handouts with discussion questions
Ethics decision framework worksheet

review.value: Software engineers shall act consistently with the public interest. When the interests of the client (ship faster) conflict with the public interest (software safety), the public interest takes precedence. This is not optional; it is the first principle.

Bias in training data produces biased models. This is not a purely technical problem; it requires diverse teams, bias audits, fairness metrics, and ongoing monitoring. Claiming 'the algorithm decided' does not absolve the engineers who built it.

The most difficult ethical situations arise when doing the right thing has personal cost (pushback from management, career risk). Professional codes of ethics provide backing for refusing to participate in harmful practices.

Collect the minimum data necessary, store it encrypted, delete it when no longer needed, and give users control over their data. These are not nice-to-haves; they are professional obligations under GDPR, CCPA, and ethical practice.

learning_objectives: Synthesize all course topics into a coherent understanding of the software engineering discipline
Present and defend capstone project design decisions using SE principles
Evaluate peer projects against software engineering best practices
Prepare for professional software engineering practice with a personal development plan
readings: Review all course materials and lecture notes
McConnell, Professional Software Development, Ch. 1-3
Prepare capstone project presentation and written defense

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session integrates everything we have covered across the semester into a coherent framework for professional software engineering practice. From process models that structure how we work, to requirements engineering that ensures we build the right thing, to architecture and design patterns that ensure we build it well, to testing and CI/CD that ensure we build it reliably, to DevOps practices that ensure we operate it effectively, to ethics that ensure we build it responsibly — these are not isolated topics but interconnected practices that reinforce each other. A team that practices TDD produces code that is inherently testable, which makes CI/CD pipelines reliable, which enables frequent deployment, which enables rapid feedback from users, which informs better requirements for the next iteration. A team that skips testing builds untestable code that breaks in deployment, creating fear of change that slows development, frustrating users who wait months for fixes. The capstone project presentations today are your opportunity to demonstrate not just technical skill but engineering judgment: the ability to make design decisions, defend them with reasoning, acknowledge trade-offs, and articulate what you would do differently with more time or different constraints.
key_concepts: Full SDLC integration: requirements flow into architecture, architecture constrains implementation, implementation is verified by testing, testing gates CI/CD, CI/CD enables deployment, monitoring feeds back into requirements
Engineering judgment: choosing the right process, architecture, pattern, and level of testing for the specific project context rather than applying one-size-fits-all practices
Trade-off analysis: every engineering decision involves trade-offs; professional engineers articulate what they gained and what they sacrificed with each decision
Capstone project defense: presenting design decisions with technical justification, demonstrating working software, explaining testing strategy, and responding to technical questions from peers
Peer evaluation: reviewing peer projects using criteria from the course (architecture quality, test coverage, CI/CD pipeline, code review evidence, requirements traceability)
Continuous improvement: the retrospective mindset applied to your own professional practice — identifying skills to develop, areas of weakness to address, and emerging technologies to learn
Professional development plan: setting concrete goals for the next 6-12 months (contribute to open source, obtain certifications, learn new architectural patterns, practice system design interviews)

Industry landscape: current trends in software engineering (platform engineering, AI-assisted development, infrastructure as code maturity, observability-driven development) and how course foundations enable engaging with them

Career paths in SE: individual contributor (senior engineer, staff engineer, principal engineer), management (engineering manager, director, VP), specialized roles (SRE, DevOps engineer, security engineer, architect)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Each team presents their capstone project (10 minutes): demonstrate working software, walk through architecture with diagram, show CI/CD pipeline in action, display test coverage report, and discuss the most challenging design decision and what they would change in hindsight. Instructor provides live feedback connecting project decisions to course concepts. Class votes on best architecture, best test suite, and best process adherence.
setup_requirements: Presentation slides or live demo environment prepared
Capstone project deployed and accessible
CI/CD dashboard visible for demonstration
Peer evaluation rubric distributed

review.value: Software engineering practices are not independent checkboxes. They form a reinforcing system: good requirements enable good architecture, good architecture enables good testing, good testing enables confident deployment, confident deployment enables rapid feedback.

There is no single correct architecture, process, or tool for every project. The mark of a professional engineer is the ability to evaluate context, choose appropriately, and articulate the reasoning behind the choice.

Technology changes continuously, but fundamentals (testing, architecture, process discipline, ethics) are durable. Invest in fundamentals first, then layer specific technologies on top. A strong foundation enables rapid adaptation.

The most valuable part of a project retrospective is identifying what you would do differently. This requires honest self-assessment and the humility to acknowledge mistakes. Every project teaches something; capture that knowledge.

quizzes.topic: Process Models, Requirements, and System Modeling

questions.question: Write three user stories with acceptance criteria for an online food ordering system. Each story should follow the 'As a [role], I want [feature], so that [benefit]' format with at least two Given/When/Then acceptance criteria.
questions.answer:
1. As a customer, I want to search restaurants by cuisine type, so that I can find food I enjoy.
  - Given I am on the home page, When I select 'Italian' from the cuisine filter, Then only Italian restaurants are displayed.
