  - Given no restaurants match my filter, When the results load, Then I see 'No restaurants found' with a suggestion to broaden my search.

2. As a customer, I want to add items to my cart from a restaurant menu, so that I can build my order.
  - Given I am viewing a restaurant menu, When I click 'Add to Cart' on an item, Then the item appears in my cart with quantity 1.
  - Given I add an item that is already in my cart, When the cart updates, Then the quantity increments by 1 rather than adding a duplicate.

3. As a restaurant owner, I want to mark items as unavailable, so that customers do not order items I cannot prepare.
  - Given I am in the menu management page, When I toggle an item to 'unavailable', Then the item appears greyed out on the customer-facing menu.
  - Given an item in a customer's cart becomes unavailable, When the customer views their cart, Then a warning is displayed and the item is removed.

rubric.key: Three properly formatted user stories with role, feature, and benefit
At least two Given/When/Then acceptance criteria per story
Stories are testable, appropriately scoped, and satisfy INVEST criteria

questions.question: Draw a UML class diagram for a library management system with at least four classes. Show attributes, operations, and relationships (association with multiplicity, inheritance, or composition). Explain one design decision you made.
questions.answer:
Classes: Library (1) --contains--> (*) Book, Book (1) --has--> (*) Copy, Member (1) --borrows--> (0..5) Copy, Librarian extends Member.

Library: name, address | addBook(), removeMember()
Book: isbn, title, author, publishedYear | getAvailableCopies()
Copy: copyId, condition, status(available/borrowed/reserved) | checkout(), returnCopy()
Member: memberId, name, email, memberSince | borrowCopy(copy), returnCopy(copy), getActiveBorrowings()
Librarian extends Member: employeeId, role | addBook(book), processReturn(copy)

Composition between Library and Book (books cannot exist without the library). Association between Member and Copy for borrowings with 0..5 multiplicity (a member can borrow at most 5 copies). Inheritance: Librarian is-a Member with additional administrative operations.

Design decision: I separated Book (the bibliographic record) from Copy (a physical instance) because a library may have multiple copies of the same book, each with its own availability status. This avoids duplicating title/author/isbn for each physical copy.

rubric.key: At least four classes with attributes and operations
Correct relationship types (association, composition, or inheritance) with multiplicities
Clear explanation of a design decision with reasoning

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Agile over Waterfall for a startup building a new product? What is one scenario where Waterfall might be more appropriate? Justify both answers.
questions.answer:
Agile's main advantage for startups is the ability to incorporate customer feedback after each iteration, reducing the risk of building the wrong product. Requirements for new products are inherently uncertain, and Agile embraces this by delivering working software frequently and adjusting direction based on real user feedback. The cost of learning that a feature is wrong after a 2-week sprint is far less than learning after 6 months of Waterfall development.

Waterfall may be more appropriate for safety-critical systems (medical devices, aviation software) where requirements are well-understood, regulatory documentation is mandatory at each phase, and changes after implementation are extremely costly. These domains benefit from the thorough upfront requirements and design phases of Waterfall because the cost of getting the design wrong (patient harm, regulatory rejection) vastly exceeds the cost of upfront analysis.

rubric.key: Explains Agile advantage with specific reasoning about feedback and uncertainty
Valid Waterfall scenario with justification about requirements stability or regulatory needs
Clear, well-articulated answer with concrete examples

questions.question: A ride-sharing startup asks you to choose between a monolithic architecture and microservices. The team has 4 developers, the product has not launched yet, and requirements are changing weekly. What architecture do you recommend and why? What would change your recommendation?
questions.answer:
I recommend a well-structured monolith (modular monolith). With 4 developers, pre-launch, and rapidly changing requirements, the overhead of microservices (network latency, distributed transactions, service discovery, independent deployment pipelines, debugging across services) far exceeds the benefits. A monolith lets the small team move fast, refactor across module boundaries easily, and deploy a single artifact.

The key is to structure the monolith with clear module boundaries (rider module, driver module, matching module, payment module) so that extracting a service later is straightforward.

I would change my recommendation when: (1) the team grows to 15+ developers and deployment contention becomes a bottleneck, (2) a specific module has dramatically different scaling requirements (e.g., the matching engine needs 100x the compute of the admin dashboard), or (3) a module needs independent deployment for compliance reasons (e.g., payment processing for PCI).

rubric.key: Recommends monolith with clear reasoning about team size and requirements volatility
Addresses modular structure within the monolith for future extraction
Identifies specific conditions that would change the recommendation

questions.question: Describe the three stages of a CI pipeline and what each stage catches that the previous one does not. Give a specific example of a bug caught at each stage.
questions.answer:
1. Lint/Format: Catches style violations, unused imports, and simple errors (undefined variables, unreachable code). Runs in seconds. Example bug: a variable named 'reuslt' that passes syntax checking but is flagged by a spell-checking linter, revealing a typo that would cause a ReferenceError at runtime.

2. Unit Tests: Catches logic bugs in individual functions and classes. Runs in seconds to minutes. Catches bugs that pass linting because they are syntactically valid but semantically wrong. Example bug: a discount calculation function that returns 0.1 instead of 10 (a percentage vs decimal error) — syntactically valid but logically incorrect.

3. Integration Tests: Catches interface mismatches between components, database schema issues, and API contract violations. Runs in minutes. Catches bugs where each unit works correctly in isolation but fails when connected. Example bug: an API endpoint returns dates as ISO strings but the frontend parser expects Unix timestamps — each component's unit tests pass but integration fails.

rubric.key: Names three pipeline stages correctly with purpose
Specific bug example for each stage
Explains the incremental value of each stage over the previous

questions.question: Explain the TDD Red-Green-Refactor cycle. Write the first three test cases you would use to TDD a function that converts a Roman numeral string to an integer (e.g., 'XIV' -> 14). For each test, state what code change it forces.
questions.answer:
Red-Green-Refactor: (1) Red: write a test for behavior that does not exist yet — it fails. (2) Green: write the simplest code that makes the test pass, even if it is hardcoded. (3) Refactor: improve the code structure while keeping all tests green.

Test 1: assert romanToInt('I') == 1
  Forces: creating the function, returning 1. Simplest green: return 1.

Test 2: assert romanToInt('V') == 5
  Forces: handling a different character. Simplest green: if input == 'I' return 1, if input == 'V' return 5 (or a lookup map).

Test 3: assert romanToInt('II') == 2
  Forces: handling multi-character input by iterating and summing values. Simplest green: iterate through characters, look up each value in a map, sum them. This generalizes the solution from single-character lookup to iterative processing.

rubric.key: Correctly explains Red-Green-Refactor cycle
Three valid test cases in logical progression
Each test states the specific code change it forces
Shows simplest-possible Green step for each test

questions.question: You are reviewing a pull request that adds 450 lines of code in a single commit. The code works correctly. What feedback do you give, and what specific improvements do you suggest?
questions.answer:
Feedback: The code works, but a 450-line PR is too large for effective review. Research shows review effectiveness drops significantly above 200 lines because reviewers lose focus and start skimming. I would approve with the request to split future changes.

Specific suggestions:
1. Split into smaller PRs: Separate the database migration, service layer, API endpoint, and tests into individual PRs that can be reviewed independently.
2. Add intermediate commits: Even within a PR, each commit should represent one logical change (extract helper, add validation, implement core logic) so reviewers can follow the development progression.
3. Write a PR description: Explain what the change does, why it is needed, and any design decisions. A 450-line PR without context forces the reviewer to reverse-engineer the intent from the code.
4. For the current PR: I would review it but note that my confidence in catching all issues is lower due to the size, and suggest we pair-review the critical sections.

rubric.key: Identifies 450 lines as too large for effective review with reasoning
Suggests splitting into smaller PRs with specific examples of how to split
Professional and constructive tone that balances pragmatism with improvement

questions.question: Your Scrum team's velocity has been 30 story points for the last 4 sprints. Management asks the team to commit to 45 points this sprint because of a deadline. As Scrum Master, how do you respond? Justify your answer using Agile principles.
questions.answer:
I would explain that velocity is a measurement of historical capacity, not a dial that can be turned up. Committing to 45 points when the team consistently delivers 30 would mean either: (1) the team cuts corners (no tests, no code review), accumulating technical debt that slows future sprints, or (2) the team fails to deliver, damaging trust and morale.

Instead, I would facilitate a conversation about options:
1. Reduce scope: Work with the Product Owner to identify which stories are Must Have for the deadline and defer the rest. If 30 points of the highest-value work ships on time, that is better than 45 points of incomplete work.
2. Extend the deadline: If all 45 points are truly required, the honest forecast is two sprints, not one.
3. Remove impediments: Are there blockers I can remove that would genuinely increase the team's throughput (waiting on dependencies, environment issues, unclear requirements)?

The Agile principle at stake is sustainable development: the team should maintain a constant pace indefinitely. Sprint commitments based on pressure rather than capacity lead to burnout, quality problems, and ultimately slower delivery.

rubric.key: Explains velocity as measurement, not target
Proposes actionable alternatives (scope reduction, timeline adjustment, impediment removal)
References Agile principles (sustainable pace, working software, honest forecasting)

questions.question: Define the 'Definition of Done' concept in Scrum. Write a realistic Definition of Done for a web application development team. Explain what happens when a team does not have a clear Definition of Done.
questions.answer:
Definition of Done (DoD) is a shared checklist that every product backlog item must satisfy before it can be considered complete and potentially releasable. It creates a shared quality standard across the team.

Realistic DoD for a web app team:
- Code is peer-reviewed and approved by at least one other developer
- All unit tests pass with at least 80% line coverage for new code
- Integration tests pass for affected API endpoints
- No lint errors or static analysis warnings
- CI pipeline is green on the feature branch
- Feature is deployed to the staging environment and manually verified
- API documentation is updated for any new or changed endpoints
- Database migrations are reversible
- Product Owner has verified acceptance criteria are met on staging

Without a clear DoD, teams accumulate 'undone work' — stories that are 'done' in name but missing tests, documentation, or deployment. This creates a false sense of progress: the burndown chart looks good but the software is not releasable. Eventually, the team must do a 'hardening sprint' to finish the undone work, which is unpredictable and often reveals integration problems that should have been caught earlier.

rubric.key: Correctly defines DoD as a shared quality checklist for releasable increments
Realistic DoD with at least 6 concrete, verifiable items
Explains consequences of missing DoD (undone work, false progress, hardening sprints)

questions.question: Compare Scrum and Kanban. When would you choose Kanban over Scrum for a software team?
questions.answer:
Scrum uses fixed-length sprints with committed scope, defined roles (PO, SM, Team), and ceremonies (planning, standup, review, retro). Work is planned in batches per sprint. Kanban uses a continuous flow model with WIP limits, no fixed iterations, and no prescribed roles or ceremonies.

Choose Kanban over Scrum when:
1. Work arrives unpredictably: Support teams, DevOps teams, and maintenance teams receive work continuously. Batching into sprints is artificial when interrupt-driven work dominates.
2. The team needs maximum flexibility: Kanban allows reprioritization at any time, while Scrum protects the sprint scope. If priorities shift daily, sprint commitments are constantly broken.
3. Delivery cadence is continuous: If the team deploys multiple times per day, sprint boundaries are meaningless for release planning.
4. The overhead of Scrum ceremonies is not justified: Very small teams (2-3 people) or teams with well-established processes may find sprint planning and review ceremonies overhead-heavy.

Scrum is better when the team needs structure and predictability, when stakeholders want regular demo cadence, and when the team benefits from the reflection of sprint retrospectives.

rubric.key: Accurately contrasts Scrum (iterations, roles, ceremonies) and Kanban (flow, WIP limits, no iterations)
At least two valid scenarios favoring Kanban with reasoning
Acknowledges when Scrum is preferable

questions.question: Explain the Strangler Fig pattern for legacy system modernization. Describe three specific steps to apply it when replacing a legacy monolithic order processing system with a new microservices-based system.
questions.answer:
The Strangler Fig pattern incrementally replaces a legacy system by routing new functionality to a new system while the old system continues handling existing functionality. Named after strangler figs that grow around a host tree and eventually replace it, this approach avoids the risk of a big-bang rewrite.

Three steps for the order processing system:
1. Add a routing layer (facade): Place an API gateway in front of the legacy system that forwards all requests unchanged. This establishes the interception point without changing any behavior. All traffic flows through the gateway to the legacy system.

2. Extract the first capability: Build a new Order Validation microservice. Update the gateway to route validation requests to the new service while all other order processing continues through the legacy system. Run both in parallel initially, comparing results to verify the new service matches legacy behavior.

3. Progressively extract remaining capabilities: Build Payment Processing service, Inventory Reservation service, and Notification service one at a time. After each extraction, update the gateway routing. The legacy system handles fewer requests with each extraction until it can be decommissioned entirely.

rubric.key: Correctly explains Strangler Fig as incremental replacement, not big-bang rewrite
Three specific, ordered steps with concrete actions for the given scenario
Mentions risk mitigation (parallel running, gateway routing, gradual migration)

questions.question: Your employer asks you to ship a patient appointment scheduling system without input validation because 'we need to launch this week.' Apply the ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics to this situation. What do you do?
questions.answer:
The ACM/IEEE Code of Ethics, Principle 1 (Public), states that software engineers shall act consistently with the public interest. A medical scheduling system without input validation poses risks: SQL injection could expose patient data (violating HIPAA), malformed inputs could cause scheduling errors that affect patient care, and lack of validation could allow appointment conflicts.

My response:
1. Quantify the risk: Explain to my employer specifically what could go wrong — data breach fines ($50K-$1.5M per HIPAA violation), patient scheduling errors, and reputational damage — not just 'it might break.'

2. Propose a middle ground: Implement critical input validation (SQL injection prevention, required field validation, date range validation) within the launch timeline. Defer cosmetic validation (phone number formatting, address normalization) to a fast follow-up release.

3. Document the decision: If my employer insists on shipping without any validation, I put my objection in writing (email) explaining the specific risks. This protects patients (by creating a record that may prompt reconsideration) and protects me professionally.

4. Escalation: If the employer overrides my objection and the risk involves patient safety or legal compliance, I escalate to the compliance officer or, in extreme cases, the relevant regulatory body. The Code of Ethics places public interest above employer loyalty.

rubric.key: References specific ACM/IEEE principle (public interest, product quality)
Proposes actionable middle ground rather than just saying 'no'
Addresses documentation and escalation as professional obligations
Identifies specific risks (HIPAA, patient safety, SQL injection)

questions.question: List the four DORA metrics and explain how improving deployment frequency is connected to improving the other three metrics.
questions.answer:
The four DORA metrics:
1. Deployment Frequency: How often code is deployed to production
2. Lead Time for Changes: Time from code commit to running in production
3. Change Failure Rate: Percentage of deployments that cause a failure in production
4. Mean Time to Restore (MTTR): How quickly service is restored after a failure

Improving deployment frequency drives improvement in the other three:

- Lead Time: Deploying frequently requires automating the deployment pipeline (CI/CD), which directly reduces lead time. Manual gates and approvals that slow deployment are replaced with automated testing.

- Change Failure Rate: Frequent deployments mean smaller deployments. A deployment with 50 lines of changes is far easier to get right (and to debug if wrong) than a deployment with 5,000 lines. Smaller batches have lower failure rates because the blast radius of any single change is smaller.

- MTTR: When deployments are small and frequent, restoring service after a failure is fast: either roll back to the previous deployment (which was minutes ago, not weeks ago) or fix forward with a small patch. Large, infrequent deployments make rollback painful because many changes are bundled together.

rubric.key: Correctly names all four DORA metrics
Explains connection between frequency and lead time (automation)
Explains connection between frequency and change failure rate (smaller batches)
Explains connection between frequency and MTTR (easy rollback/fix-forward)

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for sprint planning, reviews, and retrospectives. More than 2 unexcused absences from team ceremonies may result in grade reduction. Lecture attendance is expected but not graded.
course_policies.late_submissions: Sprint deliverables have firm deadlines aligned with sprint boundaries and cannot be submitted late. Individual assignments lose 10% per day, up to 5 days. No submissions accepted after 5 days.

course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be original team work or properly attributed open-source. Using code from other teams, previous semesters, or AI assistants without attribution constitutes plagiarism. AI tools may be used as learning aids but all submitted work must be understood and explained by the student.
